provisions.title: FSB Key Attributes: Resolution Planning
provisions.text: Resolution authorities should develop resolution plans (also known as living wills) for all firms that could be systemically significant or critical if they fail. Resolution plans should include: a strategic analysis of the firm's critical functions and core business lines; a preferred resolution strategy (single point of entry or multiple point of entry); requirements for operational continuity in resolution; a financial analysis (valuation, bail-in execution); a communication plan; and cross-border cooperation arrangements. Resolution plans should be updated at least annually and subject to periodic resolvability assessments.

provisions.title: FSB Key Attributes: Bail-in Mechanism
provisions.text: Resolution authorities should have the power to write down or convert into equity the liabilities of a failing firm (bail-in). The bail-in tool should: follow the creditor hierarchy applicable in insolvency; not apply to certain excluded liabilities (insured deposits, secured liabilities, client assets, employee claims); provide creditors with compensation at least as favourable as in liquidation (no creditor worse off principle); and be capable of execution within a resolution weekend. Banks should maintain sufficient eligible liabilities (TLAC/MREL) to absorb losses and recapitalise the firm in resolution.

provisions.title: FSB Guidance on Continuity of Access to FMIs in Resolution
provisions.text: Financial market infrastructures (payment systems, CCPs, securities settlement systems) should establish transparent and predictable arrangements for the continued provision of critical services to firms in resolution. Key principles include: FMIs should not automatically terminate access upon entry into resolution; FMIs should have clear, publicly disclosed criteria for any decisions to suspend or terminate a participant's access; resolution authorities should have the power to direct FMIs to continue providing services; and there should be advance planning between FMIs and resolution authorities to ensure operational continuity.

provisions.title: FSB Principles and Standards on Compensation
provisions.text: The FSB Principles for Sound Compensation Practices and their Implementation Standards require financial institutions to: align compensation with prudent risk-taking (multi-year performance measurement); use deferral mechanisms (at least 40-60% of variable compensation deferred over 3-5 years for senior executives and material risk-takers); apply malus and clawback provisions (adjustment of deferred compensation based on ex-post risk outcomes); ensure the independence of risk and compliance staff compensation from business line performance; limit guaranteed bonuses; and cap variable compensation relative to fixed compensation where appropriate.

provisions.title: FSB Data Gaps Initiative
provisions.text: The FSB and IMF have developed the Data Gaps Initiative (DGI) to address data shortcomings revealed by the global financial crisis. The initiative has progressed through three phases: DGI-1 (2009-2015, 20 recommendations); DGI-2 (2016-2021, 14 recommendations); and DGI-3 (2022-2027, 14 recommendations). Key areas include: data on systemically important financial institutions; shadow banking data; securities statistics; real estate price data; cross-border banking statistics; government finance statistics; and climate-related financial data. The initiative aims to ensure that policymakers have adequate data to monitor financial stability risks.

provisions.title: FSB Evaluation of Too-Big-to-Fail Reforms
provisions.text: The FSB has conducted an evaluation of the effects of the too-big-to-fail (TBTF) reforms for systemically important banks. Key findings include: G-SIBs have significantly increased their capital and TLAC levels since the reforms; there is evidence of reduced systemic risk indicators (reduced implicit subsidies, lower G-SIB risk-taking); resolution regimes have been strengthened but cross-border resolution remains challenging; market discipline has improved but requires further enhancement; and there are ongoing concerns about the impact of TBTF reforms on market structure and competition. The FSB will continue to monitor the effectiveness of TBTF reforms.

provisions.title: IOSCO Recommendations on Client Asset Protection
provisions.text: IOSCO has published recommendations on the safeguarding of client assets by market intermediaries. Key requirements include: segregation of client assets from proprietary assets; adequate record-keeping to identify client assets at all times; restrictions on the use of client assets (prohibition on rehypothecation unless authorised by client); regular reconciliation of client asset records with custodian records; adequate insurance or bonding; and disclosure to clients about the arrangements for safeguarding their assets, including the risks of omnibus accounts vs. segregated accounts.

provisions.title: IOSCO Principles for ETF Regulation
provisions.text: IOSCO has published principles for the regulation of exchange-traded funds (ETFs). Key areas include: disclosure (portfolio composition, tracking error, total expense ratio, creation/redemption process); liquidity (authorised participant arrangements, market-making obligations, trading halts); product structure (physical replication vs. synthetic, securities lending, counterparty risk from swap-based ETFs); fair valuation and pricing (indicative NAV, premium/discount monitoring); and ETF-specific risks (flash crashes, complex/leveraged ETFs, fixed income ETF liquidity during stress). Regulators should ensure that the regulatory framework addresses the unique characteristics of ETFs.

provisions.title: Margin Requirements for Non-Centrally Cleared Derivatives
provisions.text: BCBS and IOSCO have published a framework for margin requirements for non-centrally cleared derivatives. Key requirements include: initial margin (two-way exchange of initial margin, calculated using a standardised schedule or approved internal models, subject to thresholds); variation margin (daily exchange of variation margin, no threshold); eligible collateral (cash, government securities, corporate bonds, equities, gold, subject to haircuts); segregation (initial margin must be held in a segregated account by a third-party custodian); and phase-in schedule (initial margin requirements phased in from 2016-2022 based on aggregate average notional amount).

provisions.title: IOSCO Guidance on Private Markets
provisions.text: IOSCO has assessed the rapid growth of private markets (private equity, private credit, private real estate, infrastructure, venture capital) and their potential implications for investor protection and market stability. Key concerns include: valuation practices (infrequent valuations, subjective methodologies, potential for stale pricing); liquidity management (limited redemption rights, side pockets, liquidation timelines); conflicts of interest (related-party transactions, fee structures, allocation practices); leverage (fund-level and deal-level leverage); and interconnectedness with the banking system (bank lending to private credit funds, participation in leveraged loans).

provisions.title: IOSCO Principles for Regulation of Commodity Derivatives
provisions.text: IOSCO has published principles for the regulation and supervision of commodity derivatives markets. Key areas include: market surveillance (monitoring of positions, deliveries, and price relationships); position limits and position management (to prevent excessive speculation and market manipulation); reporting requirements (position reporting, commitment of traders reports); transparency (pre-trade and post-trade transparency); delivery and settlement (ensuring convergence between futures and spot prices); and cross-border cooperation (information sharing between regulators of interconnected commodity markets).

provisions.title: Stablecoin Reserve Management Requirements
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO expects systemically important stablecoin arrangements to meet robust reserve management requirements. Reserve assets should: be composed of high-quality, liquid assets (cash, short-term government securities); be held in custody by regulated financial institutions; be segregated from the stablecoin issuer's proprietary assets; be subject to regular, independent audits and public disclosure; and be sufficient to meet redemption demands under stress (the reserve should cover 100% of outstanding stablecoins at all times). The composition and valuation of reserves should be disclosed at least monthly.

provisions.title: Core Principles for Effective Banking Supervision: Overview
provisions.text: The Basel Core Principles (BCPs) provide a framework for effective banking supervision. The 29 principles cover: supervisory powers, responsibilities and functions (Principles 1-13, covering supervisory approach, independence, cooperation, licensing, major acquisitions, corrective actions, consolidated supervision, home-host relationships); and prudential regulations and requirements (Principles 14-29, covering corporate governance, risk management, capital adequacy, credit risk, problem assets, concentration risk, large exposures, related parties, country and transfer risk, market risk, interest rate risk, liquidity risk, operational risk, internal controls, financial reporting, disclosure, and abuse of financial services).

provisions.title: BCP 1: Supervisory Authority Responsibilities and Objectives
provisions.text: An effective system of banking supervision has clear responsibilities and objectives for each authority involved in the supervision of banks and banking groups. Each authority should possess operational independence, transparent processes, sound governance, budgetary processes that do not undermine its autonomy, and adequate resources. There should be a suitable legal framework for banking supervision including: authorisation of banking establishments; ongoing supervision; power to address compliance with laws and safety and soundness concerns; and legal protection for supervisors. The legal framework should include provisions for confidentiality of supervisory information.

provisions.title: Sound Practices for Managing Risks from Digitalisation
provisions.text: The Basel Committee has published discussion papers on the implications of fintech and digitalisation for banks and banking supervision. Key areas include: BigTech entry into banking (risks from data advantages, network effects, cross-subsidisation); digital lending platforms (credit risk assessment using alternative data, partnership models with banks); open banking and APIs (data sharing, third-party access, customer consent); cloud computing (concentration risk, data security, cross-border data governance); and AI/ML in banking (model risk, explainability, algorithmic bias, supervisory challenges). Supervisors should adopt a technology-neutral, activity-based approach to regulating digital financial services.

provisions.title: Sanctions Compliance in Banking
provisions.text: Banks should maintain robust sanctions compliance programmes to ensure they do not process transactions involving sanctioned persons, entities, or jurisdictions. Key requirements include: sanctions screening systems (real-time screening of transactions, customers, and counterparties against sanctions lists); governance and oversight (senior management responsibility, compliance officer appointment); risk assessment (identifying exposure to sanctioned jurisdictions and sectors); training (regular training for staff involved in transaction processing and customer onboarding); record-keeping (maintaining records of screening results and investigations); and escalation procedures (reporting of potential sanctions matches and blocked transactions to relevant authorities).

provisions.title: G20 Principles for Digital Financial Inclusion
provisions.text: The G20 has endorsed the G20 High-Level Principles for Digital Financial Inclusion. The eight principles cover: promoting a digital approach to financial inclusion; balancing innovation and risk to achieve digital financial inclusion; providing an enabling and proportionate legal and regulatory framework for digital financial inclusion; expanding the digital financial services infrastructure ecosystem; establishing responsible digital financial practices to protect consumers; strengthening digital and financial literacy and awareness; facilitating customer identification for digital financial services; and tracking digital financial inclusion progress.

definitions.definition: A resolution tool that allows authorities to write down or convert into equity the liabilities of a failing institution to absorb losses and recapitalise the firm, thereby avoiding the use of public funds.
No Creditor Worse Off than in liquidation. The safeguard that creditors should receive at least as much in resolution as they would have received in insolvency liquidation.

A crypto-asset that aims to maintain a stable value relative to a specified asset or pool of assets (typically fiat currencies), using stabilisation mechanisms such as reserve backing, algorithmic adjustment, or a combination.

The maximum tolerable level of disruption to a critical business service, expressed in terms of duration and, where appropriate, the maximum acceptable volume of data loss or number of transactions that could remain unprocessed.

Total Loss-Absorbing Capacity. The minimum amount of instruments and liabilities that should be readily available for bail-in within resolution at G-SIBs, set at 18% of RWA and 6.75% of the leverage ratio exposure measure.

national_mappings.requirement_summary: Switzerland implements Basel III through the Capital Adequacy Ordinance (ERV) and FINMA circulars. Swiss G-SIBs (UBS, Credit Suisse until 2023 merger) have been subject to going-concern capital requirements of 14.3% RWA plus a progressive component. Switzerland was an early implementer of Basel III.

HKMA has implemented Basel III capital requirements through the Banking (Capital) Rules. CET1 minimum is 4.5%, with a D-SIB buffer for designated domestic systemically important authorized institutions. HKMA also implements the countercyclical capital buffer.

national_mappings.national_reference: RBI Master Direction - KYC (2016, as amended)
national_mappings.requirement_summary: India implements FATF CDD requirements through RBI's KYC Master Direction. Covers: customer identification using Aadhaar-based e-KYC, video-based customer identification process (V-CIP), simplified due diligence for low-risk accounts, and enhanced due diligence for PEPs and high-risk countries.
national_mappings.gap_notes: FATF mutual evaluation noted improvements needed in beneficial ownership transparency.
